scenario_name: Scenario 07 — Memory Leak in Event Listener
justification: All pass criteria met: memory leak identified as Critical with clear mechanism (no close handler, listener retention prevents GC), specific fix with close event handler calling removeAllListeners, JSON.parse without try/catch noted (rated Critical rather than Important, but still identified), safe_to_deploy is false, and explicit connection made to the 'steadily increasing memory over 3 months' symptom.

observations.text: Excellent mechanism explanation: listeners prevent GC of both CourierTracker and WebSocket, thousands of leaked objects per courier over 3 months
Specific fix with named methods, removeAllListeners, and nullification of ws reference — plus note about caller dropping references
Reproduction steps provided (connect/disconnect 100 times, heap snapshot)
JSON.parse crash risk identified with concrete reproduction and fix
Bonus finding: fire-and-forget DB write with no error handling — valid additional concern
JSON.parse rated Critical rather than Important/High — arguably defensible since it can crash the process, but the rubric expected Important or lower
Production readiness section includes actionable monitoring recommendations (RSS/heap metrics, tracker count gauge)
Singleton Map suggestion to enforce one tracker per courier is a strong architectural recommendation

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: memory leak identified as Critical with clear mechanism (no close handler, listener closures retaining CourierTracker instances, preventing GC); specific fix proposed (ws.removeAllListeners, close event handler, dispose pattern); JSON.parse without try/catch noted as High severity; safe_to_deploy is false; explicit connection made to the 3-month memory climb symptom.

observations.text: Precise mechanism explanation: closures in message/error listeners capture `this`, preventing GC of old CourierTracker instances on reconnect
Specific fix with code-level detail: ws.on('close', () => this.cleanup()) and removeAllListeners
JSON.parse crash risk identified with reproduction steps and fix including input validation
Bonus finding: fire-and-forget DB writes creating unbounded promise queues — valid additional memory pressure vector
Missing close event handler called out both as root cause of the leak and as a separate important issue for observability
Production readiness section is thorough with actionable monitoring recommendations (heap size metrics, per-courier connection count)

Old instances are never torn down — the `on('message')` an

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: memory leak identified as Critical with detailed mechanism (no close handler, listener closures prevent GC, unbounded growth proportional to reconnects); specific fix with close event handler calling removeAllListeners and dispose pattern with code example; JSON.parse without try/catch noted as Critical (exceeds the 'Important or lower' bar but still identified); safe_to_deploy is false; explicit connection to '3-month memory growth pattern' symptom.

observations.text: Excellent mechanism explanation: closure over `this` prevents GC, growth proportional to total_reconnects not active_couriers
Concrete reproduction steps including heap snapshot verification
Specific fix with code example showing both close handler and dispose pattern
JSON.parse crash identified with fix code
Bonus: identified fire-and-forget DB write as amplifying the memory issue and causing unhandled promise rejections
JSON.parse rated Critical rather than Important — arguable but defensible since unhandled throw does crash the process
Strong confidence section with clear assumptions about external orchestration uncertainty
